title: 1 hour of dating advice that will change your life
text: Sometimes it could feel like modern dating is hopeless. I mean, you hear it constantly on social media, on podcasts, and from your friends. There's no hope for finding a partner in modern society, and we're all doomed to fail and die alone. Or at least that's what the common message is these days. It's hard to meet people, it's hard to connect with people, it's hard to approach people, it's hard to say the right things, it's hard to know what you're supposed to do. You don't want to get canceled for saying the wrong thing, you don't want to look stupid, you need more Instagram clout, and the list goes on and on and on. But I think many people are looking at this the wrong way, and your interpretation of the situation is what's causing dating to feel so futile. And I think there's a way to fix it. So, let me explain what I mean by this. I'm actually no longer single for once, and have met what I can only describe as the girl of my dreams. And you know when I met her? When I stopped caring and stopped trying. That's the irony. I know, it sounds stupid, counterproductive, and stereotypical, but I promise that there will actually be some value here once we get into it. I spent years trying to find the girl for me. I did all of the things that I thought I should do. I was on dating apps, I bought Tinder premium. I know, what a loser. I was looking presentable, and dressing nice, and smelling good, and hitting the gym occasionally, and asserting my dominance, and all the stupid stuff that you hear people like Andrew Tate talking about online. And guess what? I was still alone. I rarely got matches on dating apps, and when I did, it was like talking to a wall. In fact, I think my dog could have better conversations than the stuff I experienced on Hinge, and she doesn't even speak human. It just felt so empty and meaningless, and I started to lose hope. And after years of trying, I gave up. Not saying I became all doomer and thought that all hope was lost. I just decided that it wasn't worth my time and started focusing on other things. I focused on my work and picked up some new hobbies like DJing, reading, and getting into coffee. I know, kind of pretentious, but I enjoyed it. I kind of thought that dating

title: 1 hour of dating advice that will change your life

I'm going to do what I genuinely want to do, and not live my life so that I could attract girls. Because that tends to happen when you're single. Instead of hitting the gym to look good for some random girl that I probably won't even meet, I went because it made me feel amazing. I started reading interesting books and listening to interesting podcasts, not so that I had fun facts to share with people that I may or may not go on a date with. I did it to expand my knowledge, learn new things, and entertain myself. I saved the money that I was spending on stupid nights standing around at the bar and pretending like I was going to talk to girls even though I never did, and used it to go on a trip to Greece and Croatia, which believe it or not was not as expensive as you would think. I started living my life for me. Immersed myself into the moment, hung out with friends without caring if women were there or not, and worked hard on what I was doing. I put my head down and just lived my life the way I normally should have been. And after a year of not caring about dating, I noticed something. I was becoming a more interesting person. I was becoming pretty passionate about my new hobbies. I was learning a ton of new information. I was in pretty decent shape. My Greece and Croatia trips gave me cool stories to tell, like how we hitchhiked in rural Greece at 3:00 a.m. and explored the town where King's Landing was filmed in Game of Thrones. And I was doing all of these things for me, and nobody else. And guess what? Now that I was becoming more interesting, I started to stand out. In fact, I think most people would find dating apps a prerequisite for dating, and you probably can't meet people at all unless you actively use them. But, this just isn't true. I met my girlfriend the second I got off dating apps, and I firmly believe that they are one of the things that are hurting the dating market the most, and causing the most people to feel more helpless than ever before. When you outsource your dating efforts to an app sorted by an algorithm and full of user-created profiles, you give up a very natural element of how dating has always worked. Everyone with a Tinder or Hinge profile specifically curates their image to appear in a way that they think will attract the most other people. Pictures are snapped from the perfect angle in the coolest places with the best lighting and the best vibes to show how cool and interesting you are. Your prompts and your bio are curated to showcase your humor, your amazing personality traits, and your super interesting and diverse range of hobbies. Getting the confidence to walk up to someone in public, smile at them, and say something that isn't completely moronic is a lot to ask, and even thinking about this process can be anxiety-inducing for a lot of people. What do you say? What should you do? Should you ask for her number? The list of questions goes on. But, let's take a step back here. Before you worry about any of this stuff, you need to put yourself in a physical environment where you're around people that share similar interests or hobbies. You need to be in spaces where human interaction is easy to begin with, and it'll take a lot of the guesswork out of those conversation starters. Go to coffee shops, even if you aren't a coffee shop guy, and hang out. Sit at those big community tables where there's a chance that other people are near you. Don't go out and order a coffee to go and say you tried. Don't sit in the corner facing away from everyone and say you tried. You need to put yourself in places where you can interact with other humans. Join a club of some kind, start up new hobby like rock climbing, take your dog to a dog park, go grab a beer at a brewery with a friend and sit at the bar. The options are endless. All you really have to do here is find a place where you see people just hanging out and put yourself in those places. And for God's sake, when you get there, don't go on your phone and scroll on TikTok. Don't wear headphones and listen to solace videos. Don't close yourself off. Make it so that you seem as if you're open to talking to other people, and do it often so that you have more chances. These places are where you start to see opportunities to talk to other people. text: ute, it's a lot easier to casually strike up a conversation. Ask her what kind of dog she has, how old her dog is, or something about what she's reading or wearing or listening to. Ask questions. Don't think about how you can throw a sleazy compliment in to make it clear that you like her. Don't say some stupid pick-up line that you saw on Reddit. One, she does like you and you're going to get her number and start talking. Two, she doesn't like you or isn't interested and she'll say no and won't give you her number. Or three, she'll lie and give you her number just because she doesn't want to say no because she is a nice person. Number one and three seem hard to tell the difference between, but they aren't because if she doesn't answer the first text you send her in a day or so, she probably isn't interested and it probably was option three. When we're lonely, we want to meet someone to fill that void and since dating opportunities feel so limited these days, we need to be ready at any time to make sure that we don't miss any of these opportunities. I tried to be extremely aware for years, quickly sending messages to any online dating matches, trying to come up with any excuse to swipe up on a girl's Instagram story where she remembers who I am, and looking around to see if there was anyone who seemed even remotely interested in me in public. It distracted me from things that I should have been enjoying and it was like a constant weight in the back of my mind that never really went away.
